original_abstract: The selective sequestration of carbon dioxide from industrial flue gas remains a significant challenge, primarily due to the competitive adsorption of water vapor which typically diminishes material efficiency. In this study, we demonstrate the precise molecular engineering of the metal-organic framework IRMOF-74-III to achieve high-performance CO₂ capture under humid conditions. By employing a versatile synthetic strategy involving protected amine linkers and subsequent post-synthetic deprotection, a series of functionalized structures—incorporating -CH₃, -NH₂, -CH₂NHBoc, -CH₂NMeBoc, -CH₂NH₂, and -CH₂NHMe groups—were prepared and characterized. Solid-state ¹³C NMR spectroscopy confirmed that the aliphatic primary (-CH₂NH₂) and secondary (-CH₂NHMe) amines chemically bind CO₂ through the formation of carbamic species. Adsorption isotherms and dynamic breakthrough experiments revealed that the primary amine-functionalized framework, IRMOF-74-III-CH₂NH₂, exhibits a significant CO₂ capacity of 3.2 mmol g⁻¹ and maintains its performance in gas streams with 65% relative humidity. Notably, the material showed identical breakthrough times (610 ± 10 s g⁻¹) for both dry and wet nitrogen streams without structural degradation. These results highlight the effectiveness of integrating specific covalent functionality within large-pore MOFs to overcome water competition in practical carbon capture applications.
humanized_abstract: Despite considerable efforts to selectively remove CO2 from flue gas, most recent adsorbents fail to fulfill their promise due to the adverse effect of humidity, primarily caused by competition of water vapor in the framework and on the surface. Here we demonstrate the design and synthesis of IRMOF-74-III and its use for selective CO2 capture under humid conditions. The framework is synthesized using protected amine precursors and the functionalized materials obtained by post-synthetic exchange were characterized using different analytical techniques such as elemental analysis, FT-IR, 13C CPMAS NMR spectroscopy, N2 adsorption and scanning electron microscopy. The formation of stable carbamate species with aliphatic primary and secondary amines confirmed using solid-state 13C CPMAS NMR was also confirmed. Adsorption isotherms, obtained at ambient temperature using mixtures of 5 vol % CO2 in N2, revealed a high CO2 uptake (3.2 mmol g−1) for the primary amine-grafted framework, IRMOF-74-III-CH2NH2. The material can be also tested under wet gas in dynamic breakthrough mode where a gas stream of 65% relative humidity and 5% CO2 concentration in N2 was passed over the adsorbent. The primary amine-based material removes a stable amount of CO2 along the whole column and reaches a CO2 uptake of 3.2 mmol g−1 and a total mass transfer zone length of 610 ± 10 s g−1. Furthermore, dry and wet nitrogen breakthrough curves over the primary amine adsorbent give values of the same mass transfer zone length, indicating that the adsorbent does not suffer from mass-transport diffusion in wet gas streams.

Adsorption isotherms, obtained at ambient temperature using mixtures of 5 vol % CO2 in N2, revealed a high CO2 uptake (3.2 mmol g−1) for the primary amine-grafted framework, IRMOF-74-III-CH2NH2. The primary amine-based material removes a stable amount of CO2 along the whole column and reaches a CO2 uptake of 3.2 mmol g−1 and a total mass transfer zone length of 610 ± 10 s g−1.
